proposal.reasoning: This is the initial exploration phase with no prior data. To efficiently map the parameter space and identify promising directions for optimizing density, heat of vaporization, surface tension, and dipole moment, I propose four diverse strategies: (1) Strong Cohesion: maximize Lennard-Jones epsilon values to enhance intermolecular attractions, targeting higher density and enthalpy of vaporization; (2) High Dipole: maximize dummy bead bond lengths and partial charges to increase the molecular dipole moment toward the target of 3.72 D; (3) Compact Packing: reduce atomic radii (rmin) to increase density through tighter packing; (4) Balanced Baseline: moderate values across all parameters to establish a reference point for comparison. This approach ensures broad coverage of the physically relevant parameter space while respecting constraints (dummy bonds < sigma/2, equal/opposite charges, consistent cross-term radii).

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Changing Lennard-Jones epsilon (cohesion strength) and sigma (atomic size) modulates thermodynamic properties: more negative epsilon increases density, heat of vaporization, and surface tension via stronger intermolecular cohesion, while smaller sigma enables tighter packing. Independently, the dipole moment is controlled by the separation distance between dummy beads (via bond lengths ACY_DUP_bl and DIM_DUN_bl) and charge magnitude (DUP_charge/DUN_charge). By testing high-cohesion, high-dipole, compact-size, and balanced parameter sets in parallel, we can decouple the effects of dispersion forces from electrostatics and identify which parameter classes most sensitively control the target properties.
